verdict_reasoning: LYN is a Src-family tyrosine kinase canonically resident in the cytoplasm and associated with intracellular signaling complexes downstream of immune and hematopoietic receptors. However, LYN has documented activation-induced surface display via two mechanisms: (1) degranulation-linked exposure in mast cells and basophils — LYN associates with FcεRI and Kit signaling hubs at the PM during mast cell activation and degranulation, and (2) documented palmitoylation and myristoylation that can support transient outer-leaflet association in specialized immune contexts. The NCBI summary explicitly mentions mast cell degranulation, a canonical cell-state-induced PM translocation event. LYN is also reported to have membrane-associated pools in activated B and T cells. These qualify as contextual surface display via activation-induced and cell-state-specific mechanisms rather than baseline surface residence.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Whether the documented PM association in mast cell and immune-cell activation contexts is sufficient to qualify as outer-leaflet accessibility (vs. peripheral cytoplasmic or juxtamembrane), or whether that pool is large enough and stable enough to count as a discriminating surface target. Direct surface-biotinylation or flow-cytometry evidence on intact activated immune cells would resolve this.
